query: ubrogepant clinical trial phase iii status

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Ubrogepant is given orally at a dosage of 50 mg or 100 mg. 2 hours after the initial dose, a second dose may be taken if necessary. The dose should not exceed 200 mg in 24 hours. Treating any more than eight migraine episodes in 30 days is not recommended, as its safety has not yet been established. Like rimegepant, ubrogepant is for the acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. [16] Atogepant is given orally for the prevention of episodic or chronic migraines at doses of 10 mg, 30 mg, or 60 mg daily, with or without food. Zavegepant is given intranasally at 10 mg per spray once daily.

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Leiomyomata -- Treatment / Management -- Gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonists
retrieved_docs.content: Elagolix effectively reduces leiomyoma-related bleeding. In 2 recent identical double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled, 6-month phase 3 trials (trials UF1 and UF2), 68.5% of 206 women in UF-1 and 76.5% of 189 women in UF-2 receiving elagolix plus add-back therapy achieved the primary endpoint of reduced menstrual blood loss to less than 80 mL during the final month of treatment and at least a 50% reduction in menstrual blood loss compared to baseline. [74] In these trials, for those who received elagolix alone (without add-back therapy), the same primary endpoint was met in 84.1% of 104 women in UF-1 and 77% of 95 women in UF-2. After 6 months of elagolix plus add-back therapy, no difference in BMD compared to placebo was noted, although women who received elagolix alone demonstrated BMD loss. [74]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab) are used for migraine prophylaxis in episodic and chronic migraine. Rimegepant and ubrogepant are used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. galcanezumab and fremanezumab are under development for the preventive treatment of cluster headaches.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Trials -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Once preliminary evidence from phase II trials establishes the efficacy of the drug, phase III trials are conducted to obtain final confirmation of its safety and effectiveness. This phase involves a complete examination of the risk/benefit profile of the drug, usually comparing it to commonly used treatments in a broader population (typically 1000 to 3000 subjects) over a longer period (often six months). [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine Headache -- Treatment / Management -- Acute or Abortive Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin gene–related peptide antagonists: CGRP, such as rimegepant (75 mg as a single dose) or ubrogepant, may be considered for patients who do not respond to conventional treatments or those with coronary artery disease. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Fertility Sparing Management in Uterine Fibroids -- Treatment / Management -- Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone (GnRH) Antagonists
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding efficacy, elagolix has been shown to be efficacious in reducing fibroid-related bleeding. In two recent identical double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled, 6-month phase III trials (trials UF1 and UF2), 68.5% of 206 women in UF-1 and 76.5% of 189 women in UF-2 receiving elagolix + add back therapy achieved the primary endpoint of reduced menstrual blood loss to less than 80 mL during the final month of treatment and at least a 50% reduction in menstrual blood loss compared to baseline. In those who received elagolix alone (without add-back therapy), the same primary endpoint was met in 84.1% of 104 women in UF-1 and 77% of 95 women in UF-2. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, FDA-approved drugs acting on the CGRP, or its receptor, are those used in the treatment or prophylaxis of migraine. This group includes monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, eptinezumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab) against the CGRP receptor and CGRP receptor antagonists (rimegepant and ubrogepant). In addition, atogepant and zavegepant are other receptor antagonists that have received FDA approval.

retrieved_docs.title: Andexanet Alfa -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for the use of andexanet alfa. Assess the dosing parameters for andexanet alfa. Evaluate the mechanism of action of andexanet alfa. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from andexanet alfa therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine With Aura -- Treatment / Management -- Calcitonin gene-related peptide antagonists
retrieved_docs.content: Small molecule antagonists, or gepants, are oral agents proven effective for acute migraine treatment. Though more commonly used as a prophylactic therapy, some formulations are effective for abortive use. Rimegepant, administered as a single 75 mg dose, and ubrogepant are among the available options. CGRP antagonists are helpful for patients who do not respond to conventional therapy or those with coronary artery disease. [26] Rimegepant has approval for both acute and preventative treatment. [27] Zavegepant offers an intranasal option.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Ubrogepant : The adverse effects reported during the clinical studies of ubrogepant were sedation, somnolence, and dryness of the mouth. There was an increased incidence of these adverse effects when the dosage increased from 50 mg to 100 mg.

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Leiomyomata -- Treatment / Management -- Gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonists
retrieved_docs.content: Following the elagolix trials, the protocol was replicated for 2 international, randomized, double-blind placebo-controlled, 24-week, phase 3 trials for the study of relugolix. The relugolix trials demonstrated a reduction of menstrual blood loss in 71% to 80% of participants compared to placebo and significant decreases in pain, anemia, bulk-related symptoms, and uterine volume. [76] Moreover, relugolix was shown to preserve bone mineral density. The most common adverse effects of relugolix combination therapy were hot flashes and hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetic Neurokinin-1 Receptor Blockers -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic impairment: The dosage adjustments and recommendations for drugs in patients with hepatic impairment are listed below. Aprepitant: Aprepitant does not require any dosage adjustment for patients belonging to Child-Hugh class A or B. However, the dosage adjustment for patients belonging to Child-Hugh class C is undefined. Rolapitant: Rolapitant does not require any dosage adjustment for patients belonging to Child-Hugh class A or B. However, the dosage adjustment for patients belonging to Child-Hugh class C is undefined. Netupitant/palonosetron: Netupitant/palonosetron is not recommended for patients belonging to Child-Hugh class C. Therefore, this medication should be avoided in such cases. Fosaprepitant: Fosaprepitant does not require any dosage adjustment for patients belonging to Child-Hugh class A or B. However, the dosage adjustment for patients belonging to Child-Hugh class C is undefined. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Ralinepag is a selective non-prostanoid prostacyclin receptor agonist formulated as an oral, extended-release tablet taken once daily. [101] In a phase 2 randomized placebo-controlled trial, ralinepag significantly reduced PVR compared with placebo (−29.8%; P = .03) in 61 patients receiving mono- (41%) or dual- (59%) background therapy. [102] Ralinepag was initiated at a dose of 10 μg twice daily and titrated as tolerated over nine weeks to a maximum total daily dosage of 600 μg (300 μg twice daily). Reported AEs included headache, nausea, diarrhea, jaw pain, and flushing. This agent is currently undergoing investigation in ongoing clinical trials. (NCT03626688; NCT04084678)

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetic Neurokinin-1 Receptor Blockers -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Aprepitant: Although data from human studies on the effects of aprepitant during pregnancy are unavailable, clinicians should carefully weigh the risks and benefits of this medication during pregnancy. Females of reproductive age who are undergoing treatment with aprepitant should use effective non-hormonal contraception to avoid pregnancy. In addition, it is also recommended to continue using contraception for at least 1 month after discontinuing the treatment, as aprepitant may reduce the effectiveness of hormonal contraceptives. [12] Rolapitant: Although data from human studies are unavailable, there is no known risk of teratogenicity based on data from animal studies. Netupitant/palonosetron: Based on data from animal studies, netupitant may have teratogenic risks. As for palonosetron, data from human studies are conflicting with the data obtained from ondansetron. Hence, the drug should be used with extreme caution, especially during the first trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 563
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are pharmacologic agents that belong to the therapeutic class of drugs used in hereditary angioedema. Both medications are specifically indicated for the management of hereditary angioedema attacks, targeting the underlying mechanisms of the disorder to reduce swelling and improve patient outcomes. Their inclusion in this drug class highlights their shared role in treating this rare, potentially life‑threatening condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1863
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] is a class of pharmaceutical preparations that includes the agents nedokromil and nusinersen. Both mupirocin and nusinersen produce the physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, indicating that nusinersen, as a member of N [Preparations], shares this effect with mupirocin. Recognizing the categorical relationship of nedokromil and nusinersen within N [Preparations] and the shared protein‑synthesis‑enhancing action of nusinersen and mupirocin can aid in understanding their mechanisms and guiding therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1147
retrieved_docs.content: its topical uses, ketoconazole has important drug‑interaction considerations. The medication classes dihydroergotamine mesylate and dihydroergotamine tartrate are contraindicated when ketoconazole is present, reflecting a risk of adverse pharmacologic interaction. Likewise, the calcitonin gene‑related peptide (CGRP) receptor antagonist ubrogepant is also contraindicated with ketoconazole, underscoring the need for clinicians to review a patient’s medication list before prescribing ketoconazole‑containing products. Recognizing both the extensive range of ketoconazole‑based dermatologic therapies and its contraindications with specific systemic agents is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1333
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral opioid receptor antagonists are classified as drugs for constipation, representing a therapeutic class that targets opioid receptors in the gastrointestinal tract to alleviate constipation. Alvimopan is a specific member of the peripheral opioid receptor antagonist class, exemplifying how these agents function within the broader category of constipation treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2699
retrieved_docs.content: Aprepitant is a medication that may prevent nausea, and nabilone is a drug that may be used to treat nausea. Both agents are relevant in the management of nausea, offering preventive and therapeutic options for patients experiencing this symptom.

Dihydroergotamine mesylate and dihydroergotamine tartrate are both contraindicated in the presence of ketoconazole, reflecting a class‑wide interaction risk. Conivaptan hydrochloride is similarly contraindicated with ketoconazole, and ketoconazole itself is contraindicated for use with ubrogepant. These contraindication relationships underscore the importance of reviewing a patient’s full medication profile before initiating ketoconazole‑containing therapies to avoid adverse drug interactions.
